question: Which of the following is a characterization technique used to measure Young’s modulus of a biomaterial?

options.a: Tensile test
options.b: Compression test
options.c: Three- and four-point bend test
options.d: Calculation from the stress-strain curve

explanation: Tensile test helps in measurement of tensile strength, Compression test helps in measurement of Compressive strength, three- and four-point bend test helps in measurement of flexural strength and calculations from stress-strain curve help in measurement of Young’s modulus.

question: Which of the following is a characterization technique used to measure the Ductility of a biomaterial?

options.a: Calculations from strength test
options.b: Single edge notched-beam Indirect measurement
options.c: Indentation
options.d: Cyclic stress test

explanation: Calculations from strength test help in measurement of Ductility, Single edge notched-beam Indirect measurement helps in measurement of Toughness, Indentation helps in measurement of Hardness and Cyclic stress test help in the measurement of Fatigue.

question: Constant stress test helps in measurement of ____________ for a biomaterial.

options.a: ductility
options.b: toughness
options.c: creep
options.d: fatigue

explanation: Calculations from strength test help in measurement of Ductility, Single edge notched-beam Indirect measurement helps in measurement of Toughness, Constant stress test helps in measurement of Creep and Cyclic stress test help in measurement of Fatigue.

question: Elastic modulus helps in determining the strength of a biomaterial and it is measured using indentation.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Elastic modulus helps in determining the degree to which the biomaterial can be stretched on the application of force and it reverts back to its original configuration on the release of the force and it is measured using calculations obtained from the stress-strain curve.

question: Which of the following physiochemical properties of a biomaterial might cause a cellular response that might affect the cellular motility, adhesion, survival, and differentiation?

options.a: Stiffness
options.b: Porosity
options.c: Charge
options.d: Surface chemistry

explanation: Stiffness might affect cellular motility, adhesion, survival and differentiation. Porosity might affect the maximum possible accommodation of cells in the scaffold. The charge might affect cellular uptake and spread. Surface chemistry might affect the long-term functional differentiation of cells.

question: Topography and roughness of a biomaterial affect the maximum possible accommodation of cells in the scaffold.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Topography and roughness of a biomaterial affect the cellular orientation and contact guidance of fibroblast epithelial cell lines.

question: The pore size of the biomaterial affects the long-term functional differentiation of cells.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Pore size of the biomaterial affects the cellular affinity and viability by influencing cellular movement, binding and spreading, intracellular signaling and transport of nutrients and metabolites. This a cellular response of chondrocyte cell lines usually.

question: Which of the following gel/hydrogel is formed by a physical gelation mechanism?

options.a: Polyester gel
options.b: Gelatin
options.c: CMC-g-acrylic acid
options.d: Polydimethyl siloxane

explanation: Polyester gel, CMC-g-acrylic acid and Polydimethylsiloxane are prepared by chemical gelation mechanisms. Gelatin is prepared by a physical gelation mechanism.

question: ______________is prepared by condensation which is a chemical gelation mechanism.

options.a: Polyester gel
options.b: Polyvinyl chloride
options.c: Polythene
options.d: Polystyrene

explanation: The Polyester gel is a gel/hydrogel that is prepared by a chemical gelation mechanism called condensation. Polyvinyl chloride, polythene and polystyrene are prepared by addition.

question: Which of the following is not a chemical gelation mechanism?

options.a: Condensation
options.b: Addition
options.c: Cross-linking
options.d: Weak

explanation: Condensation, Addition and Cross linking are chemical gelation mechanisms. In the Condensation mechanism, the monomers react in order to form a bond by replacing a few molecules and generation of by-products. In the Addition mechanism, the polymer is formed by simple linking of the monomers without the generation of any by-product. In the cross-linking mechanism the polymer chain of one monomer links the polymer chain of another monomer in order to form a structurally complex polymer. Weak is the physical gelation mechanism.

question: _________________ helps in maintaining the structural integrity of most of the connective tissues in animals.

options.a: Collagen
options.b: Keratin
options.c: Elastin
options.d: Mucosal layer

explanation: Collagen is found in most of mammalian tissues. Collagen helps in maintaining the structural integrity of most of the connective tissues in animals. The human body is a rich source of collagen type I-IV among more than 20 different collagen types found in the body.

question: Which of the following have a structure with three peptide chains bound with each other to form a rope-like structure?

options.a: Collagen
options.b: DNA
options.c: RNA
options.d: Peptidoglycan

explanation: Collagen has a rope-like structure in which three peptide chains are intertwined. Collagen gels are thermally reversible with limited mechanical properties.

question: Type I collagen is the dominant component in the native __________ scaffold.

options.a: bone
options.b: heart
options.c: liver
options.d: cartilage

explanation: Type I collagen is the dominant component in the native bone scaffold and plays an important role in bone formation from stem/progenitor cells through a developmental cascade.

question: Collagen can be found naturally in cartilage tissue.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Collagen can naturally be found in cartilage tissue and regulates chondrocyte adhesion and stem cell differentiation to chondrocytes through molecular interactions between the protein ligands and the cells.

question: Hyaluronic acid plays an important role in ____________

options.a: wound Healing
options.b: adhesion
options.c: migration
options.d: differentiation

explanation: Hyaluronic acid or hyaluronan is a natural material that is widely found in connective tissues. It is one of the simplest GAG components in the ECM and plays an important role in wound healing in the body.

question: Hyaluronic acid can be degraded by chitinase.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Hyaluronic acid can be degraded by hyaluronidase derived from serum and cells. However, hyaluronic acid needs a precise purification procedure to remove impurities and endotoxins that may cause an immune response or disease.

question: PVC is usually added to hyaluronic acid in order to control its mechanical properties.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Adding some functional groups to hyaluronic acid can provide a more hydrophobic material capable of chemical cross-linking with other polymers such as PEG to control its mechanical properties.

question: ____________ is one of the most common methods of modifying hyaluronic acid.

options.a: Esterification
options.b: Enzymatic modification
options.c: Hydrolysis
options.d: Trans-esterification

explanation: The esterification of hyaluronic acid is the most commonly used approach to modify hyaluronic acid. Hayff-11 is a benzyl ester of hyaluronic acid that has been commercialized and clinically approved.

question: Alginic acid is obtained from ___________

options.a: brown algae
options.b: amoeba
options.c: goldfish
options.d: phytoplankton

explanation: Alginic acid or alginate is the main structural component of seaweed and is obtained from brown algae’s extracellular matrix. It is a high molecular weight linear polymer and few studies have reported that alginic acid inhibits the HIV reverse transcriptase activity.

question: _______ and ________ are monomers of Alginate.

options.a: Sucrose and glucose
options.b: Maltose and galactose
options.c: Mannuronic acid and guluronic acid
options.d: Galactose and glucose

explanation: Alginates are a type of polysaccharide block copolymers containing monomers of sequential β-d-mannuronic acid and α-l-guluronic acid, which are distributed in the polymer chain.

question: Alginate is suitable for __________________

options.a: drug delivery
options.b: wound Healing
options.c: storage
options.d: consumption

explanation: Alginate has wide applications in TE and drug delivery due to its low toxicity, relatively low cost, and ease of chemical modification and gelation.

question: Covalent cross-linking of alginate gels with different molecules is a useful approach to control the __________ and mechanical properties of gels.

options.a: swelling
options.b: strength
options.c: toughness
options.d: porosity

explanation: Alginate has the drawback of degradation through an uncontrollable and unpredictable process to release divalent ions into its surrounding medium. Covalent cross-linking of alginate gels with different molecules is a useful approach to control the swelling and mechanical properties of gels.

question: Addition of _________ to alginate improves the cellular binding and interactions.

options.a: lectin
options.b: adenosine
options.c: collagen
options.d: elastin

explanation: Modified alginate gels with some proteins, such as lectin, have high cellular binding and interactions. Such modified alginate gels have been used to culture muscle cells and make muscle tissues.

question: Mechanical properties of alginate reduce in the presence of K+ ions.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: In a clinical trial, patients suffering from a lesion in the femoral condyle were successfully treated with the autologous chondrocytes cultured in the alginate–agarose scaffolds. However, alginate gels are relatively unstable in physiological media because of a decrease in mechanical properties of the gels due to the replacement of Ca2+ ions with Na+ or K+ ions in the polymer chains.

question: ___________ can be obtained by the deacetylation of chitin.

options.a: Chitosan
options.b: Cellulose
options.c: Actin
options.d: Fibrin

explanation: Chitin on deacetylation gives Chitosan. Chitosan can be used as a biomaterial that is biomimetic in nature, this is due to its structural similarity to the GAGs in native tissues.

question: Chitosan can be dissolved only in dilute acids because of its high porosity.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Due to the high crystallinity and a chemical structure with amino acids, only dilute acids have the ability to dissolve Chitosan.

question: Chitosan has a similar structure to Glycos-Amino-Glycans (GAGs) in cartilage.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Chitosan is a favorite scaffold material in articular TE applications because of its similar structure to GAGs in cartilage-stimulating chondrogenesis.

question: ____________ is a natural polymer derived from collagen.

options.a: Gelatin
options.b: Keratin
options.c: PVC
options.d: Polythene

explanation: Gelatin is a thermoresponsive, natural biomaterial extracted from collagen via breaking its triple-helix structure into single-strand molecules. It is more cost-effective and less antigenic than collagen.

question: ____________ consists of a network of fibrous proteins.

options.a: Fibrin
options.b: Collagen
options.c: Alginate
options.d: Gelatin

explanation: Fibrin comprises a network of fibrous proteins. The structural unit of fibrin polymer is fibrinogen, which can be derived from blood coagulated as a result of an injury.

question: Fibrin is formed by the enzymatic polymerization of fibrinogen using ____________

options.a: thrombin
options.b: keratin
options.c: calcium
options.d: hydrochloric acid

explanation: Fibrin gels are formed at room temperature through the enzymatic polymerization of fibrinogen using thrombin and its degradation rate can be controlled by aprotinin or tranexamic acid.

question: The major drawback of using fibrin is its shrinkage.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: A major drawback of using fibrin is its shrinkage in vivo. In addition, fibrin gel has limited mechanical strength particularly after implantation in vivo.

question: Silk is a natural biomaterial produced by ____________

options.a: spiders
options.b: cockroaches
options.c: ants
options.d: earthworms

explanation: Silk is a natural biomaterial produced by spiders. It has been studied intensively for a long time as a biomaterial because of its low inflammatory response, biodegradability, tunable structural and chemical properties and high mechanical properties.

question: _____________ was synthesized by the reaction of silk with lactose using cyanuric chloride as the coupling spacer.

options.a: Galactosylated silk fibroin
options.b: Sulfonated silk fibroin
options.c: Elastin
options.d: Fibrin

explanation: Galactosylated silk fibroin was synthesized by the reaction of silk with lactose using cyanuric chloride as the coupling spacer. Hepatocytes were able to attach to the galactosylated-silk-coated substrates by approximately eightfold higher than those attached to the untreated substrates.

question: Peptides are composed of amino acids.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Proteins are the main constituents of all living cells. They are composed of amino acid chains. A peptide molecule is made up of more than one amino acid linked together with a peptide bond.

question: ___________ Elastin is one of the components in natural ECM.

options.a: Chitosan
options.b: Cellulose
options.c: Elastin
options.d: Fibrin

explanation: Elastin is one of the components in natural ECM, providing elasticity to the different tissues in the body, such as the lungs, skin and blood vessels.

question: The half-life of elastin is 24 hours.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: The half-life of elastin in the body is almost 70 years, and elastic fibers are insoluble and highly stable in native tissues, preventing their ease of processing as a biomaterial.

question: What is the first step in Biomaterial-tissue interactions?

options.a: Cell Migration
options.b: Adsorption of proteins
options.c: Cell Development
options.d: Cell adhesion

explanation: The initial phase in biomaterial–tissue connection is the underlying adsorption of proteins onto the outside of the biomaterial, which, in a mix with the physical and multiple properties of the surface, is thought to prompt ensuing cell practices, for example, bond, spreading and expansion.

question: The biomaterials are expected to mimic the functions of ____________

options.a: Extracellular Matrix (ECM)
options.b: Transmembrane proteins
options.c: Cytoplasm
options.d: Cell Organelles

explanation: TE depends on platform biomaterials that will have cells from the body and, with the assistance of development factors or a bioreactor, will ideally prompt tissue regeneration. These biomaterials are subsequently expected to mirror the ECM capacities and connections with the cells and the encompassing tissues. These connections have now moved past a straightforward association with the organic frameworks to impact the natural procedures that can prompt tissue recovery.

question: _____________ is the first process involved in Biomaterial-Tissue interaction.

options.a: Foreign Body Response
options.b: Elongation
options.c: Adhesion
options.d: Replication

explanation: All in all, the nature of the cooperation among biomaterials and tissues in the body can be portrayed by two primary procedures. The foreign body response (FBR), is particularly activated on account of an implantable gadget so as to shield the body from the remote material. Once the biomaterial gadget or platform is embedded in the body, a tissue/biomaterial interface straightforwardly structures and causes a progression of incendiary reactions.

question: There is the formation of the fibrous capsule when the Biomaterial-Tissue implant is implanted in the body.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Foreign body reaction (FBR) begins with an intense stage that can last as long as a few days. During this stage, leukocytes are actuated to the implantation site, and some development elements and cytokines are created. Monocytes are likewise enrolled, which separate into macrophages. After this period, which is the situation when the biomaterial is embedded in the body, an unending outside body response is activated. This is when lymphocytes and macrophages show up, notwithstanding the advancement of veins that are essential for wound mending and tissue recovery.

question: ____________ is one of the most important aspects of biomaterial-tissue interactions.

options.a: Biocompatibility
options.b: Bioavailability
options.c: Bioequivalence
options.d: Bioluminescence

explanation: Biomaterials should be able to mimic the native cell microenvironment and to control the spatiotemporal secretion of growth factors and morphogens.

question: There are three major types of biomaterial-tissue interactions.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: There are two major types of biomaterial-tissue interactions, namely local and systemic interactions. The local interactions are the ones taking place at the implant site, e.g., inflammation, infection, tumorigenesis, etc. Systemic interactions affect the organism as a whole triggering immune responses. These two usually are the reasons for the device associated complications like biomaterial’s failure.

question: Scaffolds acquire stability and strength from the cells.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: The scaffolds provide mechanical integrity and stability, the adhesive substrate, and soluble factors for the cells in newly formed tissues.

question: ____________ polymers are those which are human-made polymers.

options.a: Hydrogel
options.b: Biodegradable
options.c: Natural
options.d: Synthetic

explanation: Synthetic polymers are those which are human-made polymers. Polymers are those which consist of repeated structural units known as monomers. E.g., polythene has ethene as a monomeric unit.

question: Synthetic polymers are sometimes referred as __________

options.a: plastics
options.b: metals
options.c: ceramic
options.d: jelly

explanation: Synthetic polymers are sometimes referred as “plastics”, of which the well-known ones are nylon and polyethylene. The polymers which are formed by linking monomer units, without the any change of material, are known to as addition polymers or also called as chain-growth polymers. All these are said to be synthetic polymers.

question: Non-biodegradable- Synthetic polymers are made from _____________

options.a: petroleum
options.b: lipids
options.c: carbohydrates
options.d: proteins

explanation: Many polymers that are commonly found in households are non-degradable as they are petroleum based polymers. In order to control the environmental issues arising due to these polymers, bioplastics have been introduced as an alternative, these are biodegradable in nature.

question: Synthetic materials owing to the bioactive properties have better interactions with the cells which allow them to enhance the cells’ performance in a biological system.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Natural materials owing to the bioactive properties have better interactions with the cells which allow them to enhance the performance of the cell in a biological system. Synthetic biomaterial guidance provided by biomaterials may facilitate the restoration of the structure and function of damaged or diseased tissues.

question: Which of the following is an example of Synthetic polymer?

options.a: Polylactic acid
options.b: Dextran
options.c: Chitin
options.d: Amylose

explanation: Polylactic acid is an example of synthetic polymers. Dextran, chitin and amylose are examples of natural polymers.

question: Polyesters are a kind of Natural polymer.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Polyesters are the largest family of synthetic materials under study. The most commonly used polyesters are poly (lactic acid) (PLA), poly (α-hydroxy acids), poly (glycolic acid) (PGA) etc.

question: Synthetic polymers can be manufactured with additional chemical functional groups to induce tissue regeneration.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Synthetic polymers are attractive because they can be fabricated into various shapes. Synthetic polymers can be manufactured with additional chemical functional groups to induce tissue regeneration.

question: Polylactides are non-biodegradable in nature.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Generally, the degradation of polylactides involves random hydrolysis of the ester bonds. Poly (lactic acid) degrades to form lactic acid which is normally present in the body.

question: Hydrogels consist of _________ functional groups.

options.a: hydrophilic
options.b: hydrophobic
options.c: polar
options.d: positively charged

question: __________ is a network of polymer chains that are hydrophilic.

options.a: Hydrogel
options.b: Alginate
options.c: Starch
options.d: Amylose

explanation: A hydrogel is a network of polymer chains that are hydrophilic, sometimes found as a colloidal gel in which water is the dispersion medium. A 3-D solid results from the hydrophilic polymer chains being held together by cross-links.

question: Hydrogel forming natural polymers includes both proteins and _____________

options.a: Polysaccharides
options.b: Lipids
options.c: DNA
options.d: Nucleic acids

explanation: Hydrogel forming natural polymers includes both proteins such as collagen and gelatin and polysaccharides such as agarose, alginate, and starch.

question: Hydrogels cannot be shrunk or expanded.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Hydrogels can be designed with controllable responses as to shrink or expand with changes in external environmental conditions like physical and chemical stimuli.

question: ____________ hydrogels are referred to polymer network derived from a single species of monomer.

options.a: Homoplymeric
options.b: Heteropolymeric
options.c: Natural
options.d: Synthetic

explanation: The polymer networks obtained from a single monomer (basic structural unit of a polymeric network) species are referred to as Homopolymeric hydrogels.

question: Hydrogels can be classified into two groups based on their polymeric composition.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Hydrogels can be classified into three groups based on their polymeric composition as homopolymeric, heteropolymeric and multipolymeric interpenetrating polymeric hydrogels.

question: Hydrogels can be classified into two groups based on their configuration.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: Hydrogels can be classified into three groups based on their polymeric composition. This kind of classification depends on the physical structure and chemical composition into amorphous, semi-crystalline and crystalline hydrogels.

question: Which of the following is a group under the classification of hydrogels based on the network’s electrical charge?

options.a: Nonionic
options.b: Synthetic
options.c: Natural
options.d: Amorphous

explanation: Nonionic hydrogels are a category under the classification of hydrogels based on the network’s electrical charge. Natural and synthetic hydrogels are categories under the classification of hydrogels based on their origin or source. Amorphous hydrogels are a category under the classification of hydrogels based on the polymeric structure.

question: An ideal hydrogel material must have a high absorption capacity in a sugar saturated solution.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: An ideal hydrogel material must have the highest absorption capacity (maximum equilibrium swelling) in a saline environment.

question: ____________ was the earliest used bio-ceramic.

options.a: Porcelain
options.b: Gypsum
options.c: Gelatin
options.d: Cement

explanation: The clinical use of bio-ceramics in dentistry started in the late 18th century with the use of porcelain for crowns. Since the late 19thcentury Plaster of Paris and gypsum have been used as biomaterials in orthopedics.

question: __________ family of ceramics is widely used for bone defects.

options.a: Calcium phosphate
options.b: Calcium oxide
options.c: Calcium carbonate
options.d: Calcium bicarbonate

explanation: The calcium phosphate family of ceramics is widely used for bone defects and zirconia, alumina and their composites are used for hip-joints.

question: _____________ is a large class of inorganic nonmetallic materials.

options.a: Bioceramics
options.b: Polymers
options.c: Plastics
options.d: Antibodies

explanation: Bioceramics constitute an important group of biomaterials, their biocompatibility ranges from that of ceramic oxides (inert in the body) to that of resorbable materials (replaced by the body after they have assisted in repair).

question: In general, bioceramics are classified into two families.

options.a: TRUE
options.b: FALSE

explanation: In general, we classify bioceramics into two families: bioinert and bioactive ceramics. Their classification depends on whether the ceramic can directly form an integration at the interface between the bone and the ceramic.

question: __________ and glass-ceramic contain rich CaO and P2O5 contents.

options.a: Bioactive glass
options.b: Hydrogel
options.c: Metal
options.d: Mesh

explanation: Bioactive glass and glass-ceramic contain rich CaO and P2O5 contents. One representative is the bioglass 45S5 developed by Larry Hench in 1968.

question: What is the main component of the tissues in our body?

options.a: Cells
options.b: Organs
options.c: Muscles
options.d: Cartilage

explanation: Tissues are made up of various specialized cells that are grouped together according to their function. Whereas organs consist of tissues as one of their main components along with stroma, muscles are a kind of tissue which consist of specialized cells which aid in our movement and cartilage is a type of flexible connective tissue which is found in certain regions of the respiratory tract as well as in the articulating surfaces of joints.

question: What was a loose belief on which the miasma theory was based on?

options.a: The diseases like cholera and chlamydia were caused by pollution
options.b: Microorganisms known as pathogens can lead to disease
options.c: Pneumonia is caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae
options.d: Vericella zoster is the virus that causes Chickenpox

explanation: The miasma theory is an obsolete medical theory which believed that few of the diseases like cholera were caused by pollution, a noxious form of “bad air”. The theory suggested that the cause of epidemics arose from miasma, emanating from rotting organic matter.

question: Tissue transplants usually give rise to a ______ response.

options.a: cold
options.b: immune
options.c: hot
options.d: no

explanation: The presence of foreign tissue after the transplant triggers something that is called an immune response, in which there are immune attacks (chronic rejection) on the donor’s tissue causing its degradation.

question: Tissue Engineering is a branch of Biotechnology which relies on the knowledge available in the other branches of Biotechnology like biochemistry, cell biology, immunology, and genetics.

options.a: True
options.b: False

explanation: Tissue Engineering is a field that fuels itself from the knowledge borrowed from other well-established fields like immunology, genetics, cell biology and biochemistry for the treatment of human disease.

question: _________ approach of tissue engineering facilitates the self-repair of tissues.

options.a: Substitutive
options.b: Histoconductive
options.c: Histioconductive
options.d: Analytical

explanation: Currently approaches to tissue engineering can be stratified as substitutive, histoconductive and histioconductive. The histoconductive approach facilitates self-repair which may involve gene therapy.

question: Allo-transplantation is a technique of transplantation of cells in which the cells are derived from a genetically identical donor.

options.a: True
options.b: False

explanation: Allo-transplantation is a technique of transplantation of cells in which the cells are derived from a genetically non-identical donor, within the same species. Xenotransplantation is a technique of transplantation of cells in which the cells are derived from a genetically non-identical donor, across different species. Auto-transplantation is a technique of transplantation of cells in which the cells are derived from one part of the body to another in the same person.

question: ____________ is a very important component in tissue engineering, which incorporates the three important elements, cells, biomaterials, and growth factors.

options.a: Scaffolding Technique
options.b: Seeding
options.c: Cell culture technique
options.d: Cell isolation technique

explanation: Scaffolding is a very important component in tissue engineering, which incorporates the three important elements, cells, biomaterials and growth factors. Whereas seeding is the step following scaffolding in which the cells are cultured on a framework of the tissue. The cells to be seeded on the scaffold are isolated and cultured according to the type and need.

question: ___________ are the favorite cell type in tissue engineering as they don’t evoke an immune response.

options.a: Autologous cells
options.b: Differentiated stem cells
options.c: Heterogeneous cells
options.d: Fibroblasts

explanation: Autologous cells are considered the favorite kind of cells in tissue engineering as they do not evoke an immune response when administered. They also eliminate the need for immunosuppressants. Heterologous cells are derived from a genetically non-identical donor might cause a possible immune response. Differentiated stem cells and fibroblast cells could be either autologous or heterologous based on the source they are derived from.

question: Which organ cannot be transplanted at present?

options.a: Brain
options.b: Heart
options.c: Lungs
options.d: Pancreas

explanation: Brain is an example of the organs that cannot be transplanted in today’s date, but heart, lungs, and pancreas can be transplanted provided a donor is available, with appropriate compatibility like the same blood group, similar age, etc.

question: If a person is going to receive stem cell transplantation, they need to go through chemotherapy and other radiation procedures as a part of the preparation for the transplantation.

options.a: True
options.b: False

explanation: Yes, if a person is going to receive stem cell transplantation, they need to go through chemotherapy and other radiation procedures as a part of the preparation for the transplantation. This is done to destroy the defective stem cells in the part of the body from where the Adult stem cells are going to be derived.

question: A bone marrow donor for bone marrow transplant is compulsorily supposed to be a relative.

options.a: True
options.b: False

explanation: No, the bone marrow donor for bone marrow transplant procedure doesn’t necessarily have to be a relative. But, the siblings are bound to have more compatibility than the parents and are a better option to be considered if no other donor is available.

question: __________ is considered as a miraculous solution to Organ shortages.

options.a: Tissue Engineering
options.b: Xeno-transplantation
options.c: Iso-transplantation
options.d: Allo-transplantation

explanation: Tissue Engineering is considered as a miraculous solution to Organ shortage, as we no more have to wait for a compatible donor to die. On the other hand Xeno-transplantation, Iso-transplantation and Allo-transplantation involve donors from whom the particular organ concerned can only be taken when they die.

question: Which of the following is a major focus of a technique of tissue engineering called autologous chondrocyte transplantation (ACT)?

options.a: Cartilage
options.b: Bone
options.c: Skin
options.d: Stem Cells

explanation: Cartilage is the main focus of the technique named autologous chondrocyte transplantation (ACT). In the ACT, chondrocytes are isolated from the biopsy of healthy cartilage and cultured in vitro for a few weeks to reach a sufficient number of cells.

question: Which feature is most directly required for a scaffold to support tissue regeneration rather than simply occupy space?

options.a: High density and complete impermeability
options.b: A structure that supports cell attachment, migration, and extracellular matrix formation
options.c: Maximum stiffness regardless of target tissue
options.d: A surface that prevents all protein adsorption

explanation: A tissue engineering scaffold should provide a temporary structure that supports cell attachment, migration, proliferation, and extracellular matrix deposition. Complete impermeability or excessive stiffness may prevent tissue integration.

question: Why is scaffold porosity important in tissue engineering applications?

options.a: It prevents cells from entering the scaffold
options.b: It allows nutrient transport, waste removal, and cell infiltration
options.c: It makes all scaffolds mechanically stronger
options.d: It eliminates the need for vascularisation

explanation: Porosity is important because pores allow cells to migrate into the scaffold and permit diffusion of nutrients, oxygen, and waste products. However, high porosity can reduce mechanical strength, so a balance is required.

question: Which statement best explains why scaffold degradation rate must be controlled?

options.a: The scaffold should always degrade before cells attach
options.b: The scaffold should remain permanently without any tissue replacement
options.c: The scaffold should degrade at a rate that matches new tissue formation
options.d: The scaffold should degrade only when exposed to ultraviolet light

explanation: An appropriate degradation rate allows the scaffold to provide support during early regeneration and gradually transfer load or space to newly formed tissue. Too rapid or too slow degradation can impair regeneration.

question: Which material property is especially important when designing a scaffold for load-bearing bone repair?

options.a: Mechanical strength and stiffness suitable for the bone environment
options.b: Complete optical transparency
options.c: Ability to dissolve instantly in water
options.d: Absence of any pore structure

explanation: For load-bearing bone repair, the scaffold must have sufficient mechanical properties to tolerate the local mechanical environment while supporting bone tissue formation. Optical transparency is not the main design requirement.

question: Which statement best describes the role of growth factors in tissue engineering scaffolds?

options.a: They mainly act as structural fillers with no biological function
options.b: They can provide biochemical signals that influence cell behaviour and tissue formation
options.c: They are used only to increase scaffold density
options.d: They prevent all immune responses after implantation

explanation: Growth factors can regulate cell proliferation, migration, differentiation, and matrix production. They do not simply act as structural fillers and they do not guarantee absence of immune response.

question: Which option best explains a key limitation of using natural polymers as scaffolds?

options.a: They never interact with cells
options.b: They always have identical mechanical properties between batches
options.c: They may have weaker mechanical properties and batch-to-batch variability
options.d: They cannot be degraded by biological systems

explanation: Natural polymers can provide biological cues for cells, but they may have limited mechanical strength, variable composition, and possible immunogenicity depending on source and processing.

question: Which option best explains a key advantage of synthetic polymers in scaffold design?

options.a: Their chemistry, degradation rate, and mechanical properties can often be controlled during fabrication
options.b: They always contain natural cell-binding motifs
options.c: They never require sterilisation
options.d: They are always more bioactive than natural extracellular matrix proteins

explanation: Synthetic polymers are useful because their structure, degradation behaviour, and mechanical properties can often be tuned. However, they may lack natural biological recognition sites unless modified.

question: Why is matching scaffold stiffness to the target tissue important?

options.a: Cell behaviour can be influenced by the mechanical properties of the surrounding matrix
options.b: Cells only respond to colour and transparency
options.c: Soft tissues always require the stiffest possible scaffold
options.d: Stiffness has no effect on tissue regeneration

explanation: Cells can sense mechanical cues from their environment, and scaffold stiffness can influence adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation. Therefore, stiffness should be considered in relation to the target tissue.

question: Which statement best distinguishes a scaffold from a permanent inert implant?

options.a: A scaffold is usually designed to support tissue formation and may degrade over time
options.b: A scaffold is always metallic and non-degradable
options.c: A scaffold must prevent all cell attachment
options.d: A scaffold is used only for electrical stimulation

explanation: A scaffold in tissue engineering is commonly designed to guide tissue regeneration and may be biodegradable. A permanent inert implant is usually intended to maintain long-term structural function without being replaced by tissue.

question: Which factor would most likely reduce successful cell infiltration into a scaffold?

options.a: Interconnected pores of suitable size
options.b: A completely closed pore structure
options.c: A surface that supports cell adhesion
options.d: Controlled degradation behaviour

explanation: A closed pore structure can prevent cells from migrating through the scaffold and can limit nutrient and oxygen transport. Interconnected pores are generally more favourable for tissue ingrowth.

question: What is usually one of the earliest events after a biomaterial contacts biological fluid?

options.a: Immediate formation of mature bone
options.b: Protein adsorption onto the material surface
options.c: Complete disappearance of immune cells
options.d: Instant degradation of the implant

explanation: Protein adsorption often occurs rapidly after a biomaterial contacts biological fluids. The adsorbed protein layer can influence later cell adhesion, inflammation, and tissue integration.

question: Why is surface chemistry important for biomaterial performance?

options.a: It can influence protein adsorption and subsequent cell responses
options.b: It only affects the colour of the material
options.c: It has no effect once the material is implanted
options.d: It prevents all forms of degradation

explanation: Surface chemistry affects interactions with proteins, cells, and biological fluids. These early interactions can strongly influence biocompatibility and integration.

question: Which statement best explains the purpose of surface modification of implants?

options.a: To change the surface response without necessarily changing the bulk material
options.b: To remove the need for any mechanical testing
options.c: To make all implants biodegradable
options.d: To ensure the implant cannot interact with tissue

explanation: Surface modification is often used to improve biological interaction, corrosion resistance, wear behaviour, or antibacterial performance while preserving useful bulk mechanical properties.

question: Which surface property is most directly related to contact guidance of cells?

options.a: Surface topography
options.b: Atomic mass of the implant
options.c: Packaging colour
options.d: Storage temperature before manufacture only

explanation: Surface topography, including grooves, fibres, and roughness, can guide cell orientation and migration. This is often described as contact guidance.

question: Which option best describes the foreign body response to an implanted biomaterial?

options.a: A biological response involving inflammation, macrophage activity, and possible fibrous encapsulation
options.b: A purely electrical reaction unrelated to immune cells
options.c: A process that occurs only in non-living materials outside the body
options.d: A response that always produces perfect tissue regeneration

explanation: The foreign body response is a host reaction to implanted materials and can involve inflammation, macrophages, foreign body giant cells, and fibrous capsule formation.

question: Which statement best explains why fibrous encapsulation can be a problem for some implants?

options.a: It may isolate the implant from surrounding functional tissue
options.b: It always improves electrical signal transmission
options.c: It guarantees full regeneration of the damaged tissue
options.d: It prevents all inflammatory reactions from occurring

explanation: Fibrous encapsulation can physically separate an implant from surrounding tissue, which may reduce integration or impair function, especially for devices that require close tissue interaction.

question: Which surface strategy is most relevant for improving bone bonding on a metallic implant?

options.a: Applying a bioactive calcium phosphate or hydroxyapatite coating
options.b: Making the surface completely toxic to all cells
options.c: Removing all surface roughness and bioactivity
options.d: Coating the implant with a non-sterile organic contaminant

explanation: Calcium phosphate or hydroxyapatite coatings can improve the bone-bonding ability of metallic implants by providing a more bone-like and bioactive surface.

question: Why can excessive wear particle release from an implant be harmful?

options.a: Wear particles can trigger inflammatory responses and contribute to implant loosening
options.b: Wear particles always strengthen the surrounding bone
options.c: Wear particles prevent macrophage activation
options.d: Wear particles are required for all tissue regeneration

explanation: Wear particles can be recognised by immune cells and may induce inflammation, osteolysis, and implant loosening depending on particle type, size, dose, and biological context.

question: Which condition-sensitive statement about biomaterial biocompatibility is most accurate?

options.a: Biocompatibility depends on the material, application site, dose, degradation products, and host response
options.b: A material is biocompatible in every situation once it passes one test
options.c: Biocompatibility is determined only by the material colour
options.d: Biocompatibility means the material cannot interact with cells

explanation: Biocompatibility is context-dependent. A material that works well in one application may not be suitable in another because tissue site, exposure duration, degradation products, and host factors matter.

question: Which type of evidence would best support a claim that a surface modification improves cell adhesion?

options.a: Quantitative cell adhesion or spreading data compared with an unmodified control surface
options.b: A statement that the surface looks smooth to the eye
options.c: The colour of the laboratory container
options.d: The name of the manufacturer only

explanation: A claim about improved cell adhesion should be supported by comparative biological data, such as adhesion counts, spreading area, staining, or related cell attachment assays against an appropriate control.

question: Which statement best describes the purpose of biofabrication in tissue engineering?

options.a: To create biologically relevant structures using cells, biomaterials, or bioactive molecules
options.b: To make only non-biological metal tools
options.c: To prevent any spatial control over material placement
options.d: To eliminate the need for cell culture in all cases

explanation: Biofabrication aims to produce organised biological or bioactive structures, often using cells, biomaterials, and biochemical cues to support tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

explanation.question: What is a major advantage of 3D printing for scaffold fabrication?

options.a: It removes the need to optimise scaffold material properties
options.b: It can provide controlled scaffold architecture and patient-specific geometries
options.c: It mainly improves scaffold fabrication by increasing polymer degradation rate
options.d: It makes scaffold performance independent of pore structure and mechanical design

explanation.explanation: 3D printing can control scaffold shape, pore architecture and spatial organisation. It can also support patient-specific designs when based on imaging data, but material properties, degradation, vascularisation and mechanics still need evaluation.

explanation.question: Which limitation is most relevant when printing cell-laden bioinks?

options.a: Cell-laden constructs require higher ceramic mineral content than acellular scaffolds
options.b: Cell-laden printing is mainly limited by the colour stability of the hydrogel
options.c: Bioink viscosity has little effect on printing stress or construct shape
options.d: Maintaining cell viability while achieving adequate print fidelity

explanation.explanation: Cell-laden printing requires a balance between printability, mechanical stability and cell viability. Harsh printing conditions, high shear stress or unsuitable bioink properties can reduce cell survival.

explanation.question: Which statement best describes a bioink?

options.a: A printable formulation that may contain cells, biomaterials and bioactive components
options.b: A prefabricated ceramic implant used mainly for articulating joint surfaces
options.c: A culture medium used for cell expansion without structural printing function
options.d: A mineralised paste designed specifically to avoid cell-material interaction

explanation.explanation: A bioink is a printable formulation used in bioprinting and may contain living cells, hydrogels, extracellular matrix components or other bioactive molecules.

explanation.question: Why are hydrogels commonly used as bioinks?

options.a: Their mineral composition usually provides high load-bearing strength after printing
options.b: Their low water content helps prevent nutrient diffusion through printed constructs
options.c: They can provide a hydrated environment resembling aspects of extracellular matrix
options.d: Their main role is to increase thermal damage during cell-laden printing

explanation.explanation: Hydrogels are often used as bioinks because they are water-rich and can provide a cell-compatible hydrated environment. However, many hydrogels require optimisation to improve printability and mechanical stability.

explanation.question: Which comparison between extrusion bioprinting and inkjet bioprinting is most accurate?

options.a: Inkjet printing is generally preferred for highly viscous continuous filaments
options.b: Extrusion printing can handle more viscous bioinks, while inkjet printing usually requires lower-viscosity droplets
options.c: Extrusion printing is mainly defined by droplet ejection rather than continuous material deposition
options.d: Inkjet and extrusion printing have the same viscosity requirements for all bioink formulations

explanation.explanation: Extrusion bioprinting can often process more viscous materials, while inkjet bioprinting typically ejects droplets and is more limited by viscosity, droplet formation and nozzle conditions.

explanation.question: Which factor is most likely to reduce cell viability during nozzle-based bioprinting?

options.a: Excessive shear stress during extrusion
options.b: Using a hydrogel formulation with appropriate cell-compatible conditions
options.c: Maintaining sterile handling during the printing process
options.d: Controlling printing temperature for temperature-sensitive bioinks

explanation.explanation: High shear stress during nozzle-based printing can damage cells. Printing pressure, nozzle diameter, bioink viscosity and printing speed must be controlled to balance cell survival and shape fidelity.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why vascularisation is a major challenge in thick engineered tissues?

options.a: Printed hydrogels generally contain mature blood vessels immediately after crosslinking
options.b: Vascularisation is mainly relevant to non-biological metal devices rather than living tissues
options.c: Cells in thick constructs are usually unaffected by oxygen and nutrient gradients
options.d: Cells far from nutrient supply may suffer from limited oxygen and nutrient diffusion

explanation.explanation: Thick tissues require adequate transport of oxygen and nutrients. Without vascularisation or perfusion, cells far from the surface may become hypoxic or die.

explanation.question: Which statement is most accurate about printing resolution in biofabrication?

options.a: Higher resolution mainly improves tissue function by eliminating material-dependent effects
options.b: Lower resolution is generally preferred because scaffold architecture has limited biological relevance
options.c: Higher resolution can improve structural detail but may involve trade-offs with speed, material choice or cell viability
options.d: Resolution is primarily a visual property and does not affect pore geometry or construct design

explanation.explanation: Printing resolution affects structural detail, but higher resolution may require smaller nozzles, harsher conditions, slower printing or more restrictive material properties.

explanation.question: Which type of question best tests whether a scientific answer avoids overclaiming in biofabrication?

options.a: A question asking for the name of one commonly used printing method
options.b: A question asking whether current printed constructs can fully replace native organs without limitations
options.c: A question asking whether a printer deposits material in a controlled pattern
options.d: A question asking for the definition of scaffold porosity

explanation.explanation: A good scientific evaluation question can test whether an answer recognises limitations and avoids unsupported claims. Biofabrication has important promise, but fully replacing native organs remains limited by challenges such as vascularisation, maturation, scale and function.

explanation.question: Which property best explains why hydrogels are often used to mimic aspects of soft extracellular matrix?

options.a: They are water-rich polymer networks that can provide a hydrated microenvironment
options.b: They are dense calcium phosphate structures with high compressive strength
options.c: They are mainly used because their low swelling improves mineral hardness
options.d: They function primarily by preventing protein and cell interactions

explanation.explanation: Hydrogels are hydrated polymer networks. Their high water content and tunable chemistry make them useful for mimicking some features of soft extracellular matrix environments.

explanation.question: Which limitation is commonly associated with many unmodified hydrogels in tissue engineering?

options.a: Their high water content usually provides sufficient load-bearing strength without reinforcement
options.b: Their hydrated structure usually prevents diffusion of nutrients and soluble factors
options.c: Their polymer network makes them unsuitable for cell encapsulation applications
options.d: They usually have limited mechanical strength compared with load-bearing tissues

explanation.explanation: Many hydrogels are biologically useful because they are hydrated and cell-compatible, but their mechanical strength may be insufficient for load-bearing applications unless reinforced or modified.

explanation.question: Why is crosslinking important in hydrogel design?

options.a: It primarily converts hydrated polymer networks into calcium phosphate ceramics
options.b: It is mainly used to remove the relationship between hydrogel structure and swelling
options.c: It can control network stability, swelling, degradation and mechanical behaviour
options.d: It generally makes hydrogel properties independent of polymer chemistry

explanation.explanation: Crosslinking controls the structure of the hydrogel network. This affects swelling, stiffness, degradation, permeability and sometimes cell compatibility.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why collagen is widely used in biomaterials and tissue engineering?

options.a: It is a bioinert metallic material used mainly for corrosion-resistant implants
options.b: It is a major extracellular matrix protein that can support cell adhesion and tissue organisation
options.c: It is a synthetic ceramic phase mainly used for hard bearing surfaces
options.d: It is selected because enzymatic degradation is not relevant to collagen-based materials

explanation.explanation: Collagen is a major ECM protein and can provide biological cues for cell adhesion, migration and matrix organisation. However, its mechanical properties and degradation may need control.

explanation.question: Which limitation should be considered when using collagen-based scaffolds?

options.a: They may show weak mechanical properties, rapid degradation and source-dependent variability
options.b: They are mainly limited by their lack of any cell-interactive biological motifs
options.c: They are usually avoided because collagen cannot be processed into scaffold structures
options.d: They are primarily unsuitable because collagen is not found in extracellular matrix

explanation.explanation: Collagen is biologically active, but collagen scaffolds can have limited mechanical strength, enzymatic degradation and variability depending on source and processing.

explanation.question: Which statement best describes alginate as a biomaterial?

options.a: A calcium phosphate ceramic used mainly for direct bone bonding
options.b: A collagen-derived protein that forms thermoresponsive gels after denaturation
options.c: A metallic alloy used in load-bearing orthopaedic implants
options.d: A polysaccharide that can form hydrogels, often through ionic crosslinking with divalent cations

explanation.explanation: Alginate is a natural polysaccharide that can form hydrogels, commonly through ionic crosslinking with divalent ions such as calcium.

explanation.question: Which is a common limitation of alginate hydrogels for cell-interactive tissue engineering applications?

options.a: Alginate gels are mainly limited because they are mineral ceramics rather than polymers
options.b: Alginate is typically unsuitable for hydrogel formation in aqueous systems
options.c: Unmodified alginate has limited intrinsic cell-adhesive motifs
options.d: Alginate has the same native cell-binding sequence profile as collagen

explanation.explanation: Alginate can form useful hydrogels, but unmodified alginate generally lacks strong cell-adhesive motifs. It is often modified or combined with bioactive molecules to improve cell interactions.

explanation.question: Why is chitosan often discussed for cartilage or wound-related biomaterials?

options.a: It is a metallic biomaterial with corrosion resistance similar to titanium alloys
options.b: It is a natural polysaccharide with structural similarity to glycosaminoglycans and can support certain biological interactions
options.c: It is a calcium phosphate mineral that bonds directly to bone under physiological conditions
options.d: It is mainly used because it lacks functional groups for chemical modification

explanation.explanation: Chitosan is derived from chitin and has structural similarity to glycosaminoglycans. This makes it relevant for cartilage, wound healing and scaffold-related applications.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why gelatin can be useful as a biomaterial?

options.a: It is a calcium phosphate ceramic mainly used for osteoconductive coatings
options.b: It is a non-polymeric salt used to crosslink alginate hydrogels
options.c: It is a metallic implant material with high fatigue resistance
options.d: It is derived from collagen and can retain some cell-interactive features while being easier to process

explanation.explanation: Gelatin is derived from collagen and is commonly used because it is relatively processable and can provide biological cues. Its stability often requires crosslinking or modification.

explanation.question: Which condition-sensitive statement about hydrogel swelling is most accurate?

options.a: Swelling depends on polymer chemistry, crosslinking density, ionic environment and water availability
options.b: Swelling is mainly determined by whether the gel is transparent after fabrication
options.c: Swelling generally has little relationship with diffusion or mechanical behaviour
options.d: Swelling is best evaluated without considering the surrounding fluid environment

explanation.explanation: Hydrogel swelling is context-dependent. Polymer chemistry, crosslink density, pH, ionic strength and surrounding fluid conditions can all influence swelling and mechanical properties.

explanation.question: Which option best compares natural and synthetic hydrogels for tissue engineering?

options.a: Natural hydrogels mainly offer mechanical uniformity, while synthetic hydrogels mainly provide native biological motifs
options.b: Synthetic hydrogels are generally selected because their chemistry cannot be modified
options.c: Natural hydrogels may provide biological cues, while synthetic hydrogels often offer more controllable chemistry and mechanics
options.d: Natural and synthetic hydrogels are compared mainly by optical transparency rather than biological or mechanical function

explanation.explanation: Natural hydrogels can provide biological recognition signals, while synthetic hydrogels may offer better control over structure, degradation and mechanics. Both categories have advantages and limitations.

explanation.question: Which evidence would best support a claim that a hydrogel improves chondrogenic differentiation?

options.a: Improved optical transparency and ease of handling during culture
options.b: Increased cartilage-related markers compared with an appropriate control group
options.c: Lower polymer concentration without assessment of cell phenotype
options.d: Higher swelling ratio without matrix or gene-expression evidence

explanation.explanation: Claims about chondrogenic differentiation should be supported by relevant markers, such as cartilage matrix production or gene/protein expression, compared with proper controls.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why degradability can be beneficial in a hydrogel scaffold?

options.a: Degradability is mainly useful because it prevents matrix deposition by cells
options.b: A degradable hydrogel should lose support before cell attachment begins
options.c: Degradation is beneficial when it makes the scaffold independent of tissue formation rate
options.d: Controlled degradation can allow gradual replacement by newly formed tissue

explanation.explanation: Controlled degradation can help a scaffold provide early support and later create space for tissue deposition and remodelling. The degradation rate must match the intended application.

explanation.question: Which feature is most important when using a hydrogel for controlled drug delivery?

options.a: Ability to regulate drug loading and release kinetics
options.b: High swelling without considering drug-polymer interactions
options.c: Rapid degradation before drug loading is completed
options.d: Mechanical stiffness alone, regardless of release profile

explanation.explanation: Hydrogels can be designed for drug delivery by controlling swelling, mesh size, degradation and interactions with the drug, which influence release kinetics.

explanation.question: Which uncertainty should be recognised when asked whether one hydrogel is superior to another?

options.a: A hydrogel with higher swelling should be considered superior regardless of tissue type
options.b: A natural hydrogel should be considered superior whenever biological cues are present
options.c: Superiority depends on application, cell type, mechanical requirements, degradation and outcome measures
options.d: A synthetic hydrogel should be considered superior whenever its chemistry is more controllable

explanation.explanation: Material comparison is context-dependent. A careful answer should avoid overclaiming and recognise that the best hydrogel depends on the intended tissue, cell type, mechanical demands, degradation profile and evaluation metrics.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why hydroxyapatite is used in bone-related biomaterials?

options.a: It is used mainly because its hydrated polymer network resembles soft extracellular matrix
options.b: It chemically resembles the mineral phase of bone and can support bone bonding
options.c: It is primarily selected because it behaves like a flexible elastomer under load
options.d: It improves bone repair mainly by avoiding interaction with mineralised tissue

explanation.explanation: Hydroxyapatite is chemically similar to the mineral component of bone, which helps explain its use in bone repair, coatings and osteoconductive biomaterials.

explanation.question: Which limitation is most relevant for using hydroxyapatite as a structural load-bearing implant by itself?

options.a: Its similarity to bone mineral reduces its relevance for bone-contact applications
options.b: Its calcium phosphate composition prevents osteoconductive surface behaviour
options.c: Its biological activity usually provides enough toughness for high-load implantation
options.d: Brittleness and limited fracture toughness

explanation.explanation: Hydroxyapatite is bioactive and bone-like, but it is brittle and has limited fracture toughness, which can restrict its use as a standalone load-bearing implant.

explanation.question: Which option best describes osteoconduction?

options.a: The stimulation of progenitor cells to differentiate into osteoblast-like cells
options.b: A material property that supports bone growth along its surface or structure
options.c: The release of soluble signals that directly initiates ectopic bone formation
options.d: The conversion of a polymeric scaffold into a calcium phosphate ceramic

explanation.explanation: Osteoconduction refers to the ability of a material to support bone growth on or through its structure. It is different from osteoinduction, which involves inducing progenitor cells toward bone formation.

explanation.question: Which option best describes osteoinduction?

options.a: The passive support of bone growth along an existing scaffold surface
options.b: The mineral dissolution of calcium phosphate without any cell response
options.c: The formation of a fibrous capsule around a bone-contact implant
options.d: The ability to induce undifferentiated or progenitor cells to become bone-forming cells

explanation.explanation: Osteoinduction refers to the ability to stimulate progenitor cells toward osteogenic differentiation. It is a stronger biological claim than simple osteoconduction.

explanation.question: Which evidence would best support a claim that a biomaterial is osteoinductive?

options.a: Evidence of osteogenic differentiation or ectopic bone formation under appropriate experimental conditions
options.b: Improved compressive stiffness without assessment of osteogenic differentiation
options.c: Surface roughness measurements showing increased nanoscale texture
options.d: Calcium phosphate content similar to bone mineral but no cell or tissue response data

explanation.explanation: Osteoinduction requires evidence that the material can induce osteogenic differentiation or bone formation, not merely that it is stiff, rough, or chemically similar to bone.

explanation.question: Which statement best compares bioinert and bioactive ceramics?

options.a: Bioinert ceramics are designed to degrade rapidly, while bioactive ceramics avoid tissue contact
options.b: Bioactive and bioinert ceramics are distinguished mainly by whether they are natural polymers
options.c: Bioactive ceramics can form bonding interactions with tissue, while bioinert ceramics are designed to have minimal chemical interaction
options.d: Bioinert ceramics primarily stimulate osteogenic differentiation through soluble growth factor release

explanation.explanation: Bioactive ceramics are intended to interact with tissue and may bond to bone. Bioinert ceramics are relatively stable and have limited direct chemical bonding with tissue.

explanation.question: Which ceramic is commonly associated with high wear resistance in orthopaedic bearing applications?

options.a: Alginate
options.b: Alumina
options.c: Gelatin
options.d: Collagen

explanation.explanation: Alumina is a bioinert ceramic known for hardness and wear resistance, making it relevant to certain orthopaedic bearing applications.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains the use of bioactive glass in bone repair?

options.a: It can form a surface layer that supports bonding with bone tissue
options.b: It functions mainly by preventing ion exchange with surrounding biological fluid
options.c: It is used because it behaves as a hydrated polymer network similar to soft ECM
options.d: It is primarily selected because it has the same mechanical behaviour as cortical bone

explanation.explanation: Bioactive glass can interact with biological fluids and form a surface apatite-like layer, supporting bonding with bone tissue.

explanation.question: Which factor is most important when selecting a calcium phosphate material for bone regeneration?

options.a: Whether the material has the highest possible density regardless of defect site
options.b: Whether the material prevents interaction with bone-forming cells
options.c: Whether the material has a fixed resorption rate in every implantation environment
options.d: Its balance of bioactivity, resorption rate, mechanical properties, and intended clinical site

explanation.explanation: Calcium phosphate selection depends on the intended application. Bioactivity, degradation or resorption rate, mechanical stability, and defect environment all matter.

explanation.question: Which condition-sensitive statement about ceramic resorption is most accurate?

options.a: Rapid resorption is preferred whenever the ceramic is used in a bone defect
options.b: Resorption can be beneficial if it matches the rate of new bone formation
options.c: Resorption rate mainly affects implant surface colour rather than bone repair
options.d: A non-resorbable ceramic cannot be useful in any bone-contact application

explanation.explanation: Controlled resorption can allow gradual replacement by bone. If resorption is too fast or too slow, mechanical support and tissue regeneration may be compromised.

explanation.question: Why are hydroxyapatite coatings used on some metallic implants?

options.a: To make the implant behave like a flexible cartilage substitute
options.b: To reduce bone bonding by masking the implant from mineralised tissue
options.c: To combine the mechanical strength of metal with a more bone-interactive surface
options.d: To convert the metal implant into a degradable hydrogel scaffold

explanation.explanation: Metallic implants provide mechanical strength, while hydroxyapatite coatings can improve bone interaction and osseointegration at the surface.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why pore structure matters in bioceramic scaffolds for bone repair?

options.a: Pores can support cell infiltration, vascular ingrowth, and bone tissue formation
options.b: Pores mainly improve performance by removing the need for biological evaluation
options.c: Pore structure is relevant to polymer hydrogels but not to ceramic scaffolds
options.d: Closed pores are generally preferred when the goal is tissue ingrowth

explanation.explanation: Porous bioceramic scaffolds can support bone ingrowth and vascularisation, although pore size and interconnectivity must be balanced with mechanical strength.

explanation.question: Which outcome would be most relevant for evaluating osseointegration of a bone implant?

options.a: A decrease in hydrogel swelling ratio after crosslinking
options.b: Improved antibacterial activity without assessment of bone contact
options.c: Reduced polymer degradation rate in phosphate-buffered saline
options.d: Direct structural and functional connection between bone and implant surface

explanation.explanation: Osseointegration refers to a direct structural and functional connection between living bone and an implant surface. It is relevant for bone-anchored implants.

explanation.question: Which statement should be avoided when evidence is limited to in vitro mineralisation assays?

options.a: Stating that the assay may suggest osteogenic potential under tested conditions
options.b: Mentioning that further in vivo or clinical evidence may be needed
options.c: Claiming that the material is proven to regenerate large human bone defects clinically
options.d: Recognising that in vitro results are condition-dependent

explanation.explanation: In vitro mineralisation can support early evidence, but it does not by itself prove clinical regeneration of large human bone defects. Scientific interpretation should avoid overclaiming beyond the available evidence.

explanation.question: Which comparison between hydroxyapatite and bioactive glass is most accurate?

options.a: Hydroxyapatite is a natural protein, while bioactive glass is a synthetic hydrogel
options.b: Both can be used in bone-related biomaterials, but their dissolution behaviour, ion release, and biological responses may differ
options.c: Both materials are used mainly because they avoid ion exchange in biological environments
options.d: Hydroxyapatite and bioactive glass are identical in chemistry, degradation, and clinical performance

explanation.explanation: Hydroxyapatite and bioactive glass are both relevant to bone repair, but they are not identical. Their chemistry, dissolution, ion release, and tissue responses can differ.

explanation.question: Which statement best describes the foreign body response to an implanted biomaterial?

options.a: A host response involving protein adsorption, inflammation, macrophage activity, and possible fibrous encapsulation
options.b: A mechanical fatigue process that occurs without immune-cell involvement
options.c: A surface mineralisation process limited to calcium phosphate implants
options.d: A sterilisation method used to remove bacteria before implantation

explanation.explanation: The foreign body response is a biological host response to an implanted material. It can involve protein adsorption, inflammatory cells, macrophages, foreign body giant cells, and fibrous capsule formation.

explanation.question: Why can fibrous capsule formation reduce the function of some implants?

options.a: It improves implant function by increasing direct molecular exchange with surrounding tissues
options.b: It is mainly useful because it prevents macrophage recruitment to the implant site
options.c: It indicates that the implant has been fully replaced by regenerated tissue
options.d: It can physically separate the implant from surrounding tissue and limit functional integration

explanation.explanation: Fibrous encapsulation can isolate an implant from surrounding tissue. This may be problematic for implants requiring close tissue contact, integration, sensing, or controlled molecular exchange.

explanation.question: Which factor is most likely to influence the severity of the host response to an implant?

options.a: The number of processing steps reported without considering material properties
options.b: Material chemistry, surface properties, degradation products, implant location, and host condition
options.c: The naming convention used for the biomaterial in a publication
options.d: Whether the implant is described before or after the methods section

explanation.explanation: Host response is multifactorial. Material chemistry, surface roughness, degradation products, implantation site, mechanical environment, contamination, and patient-specific factors can all influence the response.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains aseptic loosening of an orthopaedic implant?

options.a: Implant loosening caused primarily by acute bacterial biofilm infection
options.b: Improved fixation caused by increased mineral deposition around an implant
options.c: Implant loosening not primarily caused by infection, often associated with wear particles, inflammation, and bone loss
options.d: A normal remodelling process that indicates successful long-term osseointegration

explanation.explanation: Aseptic loosening refers to loosening without infection as the primary cause. It is often linked to wear debris, inflammatory reactions, osteolysis, and mechanical factors.

explanation.question: Why can wear particles from an implant contribute to osteolysis?

options.a: They can activate immune cells and inflammatory pathways that promote bone resorption
options.b: They generally act as osteogenic particles that directly improve bone deposition
options.c: They mainly reduce implant failure by suppressing macrophage activity
options.d: They are evaluated by measuring implant surface roughness before implantation

explanation.explanation: Wear particles can be phagocytosed or recognised by immune cells, leading to inflammatory mediator release. This can promote osteoclast activity and bone resorption around implants.

explanation.question: Which condition-sensitive statement about implant wear particles is most accurate?

options.a: Wear particles from different materials produce identical host responses once implanted
options.b: The biological effect of wear particles is independent of exposure duration
options.c: Wear particle effects are determined mainly by whether the implant is metallic or polymeric
options.d: Biological response can depend on particle material, size, shape, dose, and exposure duration

explanation.explanation: The biological effect of wear debris is context-dependent. Particle size, shape, chemistry, concentration, and duration of exposure can influence inflammatory and tissue responses.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains stress shielding in orthopaedic implants?

options.a: A low-stiffness hydrogel increases bone loading by acting as a permanent mineralised scaffold
options.b: A stiff implant can carry too much load, reducing mechanical stimulation of nearby bone and causing bone resorption
options.c: Stress shielding describes bacterial coverage of an implant surface after infection
options.d: Stress shielding mainly refers to polymer swelling under physiological fluid exposure

explanation.explanation: Stress shielding occurs when an implant is much stiffer than surrounding bone and carries a disproportionate amount of load. Reduced loading of bone can lead to bone resorption.

explanation.question: Which material design strategy could reduce stress shielding in a bone implant?

options.a: Using an implant design or material with stiffness closer to that of bone where mechanically appropriate
options.b: Increasing implant stiffness far beyond the surrounding bone in every anatomical site
options.c: Prioritising surface colour and visual uniformity over mechanical compatibility
options.d: Avoiding fatigue testing because stress shielding is unrelated to load transfer

explanation.explanation: Reducing stiffness mismatch between implant and bone can help reduce stress shielding. However, the implant must still have adequate strength and fatigue resistance for its application.

explanation.question: Which statement best describes corrosion-related implant failure?

options.a: A cell-mediated process in which collagen is converted into mineralised bone
options.b: Mechanical fatigue caused by repeated loading without chemical involvement
options.c: A surface modification method used to increase hydrogel swelling
options.d: Chemical or electrochemical degradation of a material that may release ions or weaken the implant

explanation.explanation: Corrosion involves chemical or electrochemical degradation. In implants, corrosion may weaken the material or release ions that can affect local or systemic tissue responses.

explanation.question: Which evidence would best support a claim that an implant material has good corrosion resistance?

options.a: A statement that the material has been polished before imaging
options.b: A comparison of surface roughness without exposure to simulated physiological fluid
options.c: Electrochemical testing or ion-release data under relevant simulated physiological conditions
options.d: A mechanical compression test performed without chemical or electrochemical measurements

explanation.explanation: Corrosion resistance should be supported by relevant experimental data, such as electrochemical measurements, immersion testing, and ion release under conditions that approximate the intended environment.

explanation.question: Which statement best explains why infection is a serious complication for implants?

options.a: Biofilm formation generally improves osseointegration by increasing extracellular matrix deposition
options.b: Bacteria can attach to implant surfaces and form biofilms that are difficult to eradicate
options.c: Implant-associated bacteria are usually less tolerant to treatment than planktonic bacteria
options.d: Infection risk is determined mainly by implant stiffness rather than bacterial attachment

explanation.explanation: Implant-associated infection is serious because bacteria can adhere to surfaces and form biofilms, which can resist immune clearance and antimicrobial treatment.

explanation.question: Which statement best distinguishes local and systemic responses to a biomaterial?

options.a: Local and systemic responses are distinguished mainly by whether the material is natural or synthetic
options.b: Local responses refer to drug release, while systemic responses refer only to scaffold porosity
options.c: Local responses occur near the implant site, while systemic responses affect the body more broadly
options.d: Systemic responses are limited to cell adhesion at the implant surface

explanation.explanation: Local responses include inflammation, tissue integration, infection, or fibrosis near the implant. Systemic responses may involve immune, toxic, or inflammatory effects beyond the implant site.

explanation.question: Which interpretation should be avoided when a study reports only short-term low cytotoxicity in vitro?

options.a: Stating that short-term cytotoxicity appeared low under the tested conditions
options.b: Noting that further long-term and in vivo evaluation may be needed
options.c: Claiming the material is proven safe for long-term human implantation
options.d: Recognising that in vitro findings are limited by experimental conditions

explanation.explanation: Short-term in vitro cytotoxicity data cannot by itself prove long-term clinical safety. Scientific interpretation should avoid overclaiming beyond the available evidence.
